title: What does this for loop log?
explanation: i starts at 0. The loop runs while i < 3, so it executes for i = 0, 1, 2 — then i becomes 3, the condition fails, and the loop stops. The last value logged is 2, not 3.
patternExplanation: i < length stops one before the length — the most common off-by-one to get right.

title: What is total after the loop?
explanation: The loop adds 1 + 2 + 3 + 4. Running total: 0 → 1 → 3 → 6 → 10. The <= 4 condition means i = 4 is included — a common source of confusion with < vs <=.
patternExplanation: Trace the accumulator step by step — the final value equals the sum of all values i takes.

title: How many times does this loop run?
explanation: i takes the values 0, 3, 6 — then i becomes 9, which fails i < 9, so the loop stops. That's 3 iterations. When the step size is not 1, count: (end - start) / step = (9 - 0) / 3 = 3.
patternExplanation: With i += n, the number of iterations is ceil((end - start) / n) — not the same as the end value.

title: Off-by-one: <= vs <
options: TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined
explanation: nums.length is 3. Using <= means i runs 0, 1, 2, 3. When i = 3, nums[3] does not exist — JavaScript returns undefined rather than throwing. This is the classic off-by-one bug: < is almost always correct for array loops, not <=.
patternExplanation: Use i < array.length, not i <= array.length — the last valid index is length - 1.

title: What does break do here?
explanation: break immediately exits the loop. When i reaches 3, the break fires before console.log(i) — so 3 is never printed. The output is 0, 1, 2. Note that the console.log comes after the if-check, so even i = 3 is not logged.
patternExplanation: break exits the entire loop at the point it is hit — any code after it in the same iteration is skipped.

title: What does continue skip?
explanation: continue skips the rest of the current iteration and jumps to the next one. When i is even (0, 2, 4), continue fires and console.log is skipped. When i is odd (1, 3), continue does not fire and the value is logged.
patternExplanation: continue skips the remainder of the current iteration — unlike break, it does not exit the loop.

title: How many times does the inner log run?
explanation: The outer loop runs 3 times (i = 0, 1, 2). For each outer iteration, the inner loop runs 4 times (j = 0, 1, 2, 3). Total: 3 × 4 = 12. Nested loop iteration count is always outer × inner.
patternExplanation: A nested loop runs outer_iterations × inner_iterations times in total.
